does: This paper presents an experimental study of three state-of-the-art approximate unlearning methods for linear models, systematically comparing their performance across three dimensions: efficiency (computational cost), effectiveness (quality of unlearning), and certifiability (ability to prove deleted data has been removed). The authors extend existing methods and establish a common evaluation pipeline to fairly assess these trade-offs. By implementing and benchmarking these approaches on linear models, the work provides concrete empirical evidence about how much approximation error each method tolerates and what computational savings can be achieved relative to full retraining, offering practitioners guidance on method selection based on their particular constraints.
connects: This work builds directly on the emerging literature addressing machine unlearning, which has grown in response to regulatory frameworks like GDPR and CCPA that enshrine the right to be forgotten. Prior work has proposed approximate unlearning methods that avoid full retraining by maintaining sufficient statistics or gradient information, though these approaches have lacked rigorous empirical comparison. The paper connects to broader research on efficient model updating and incremental learning, as well as to work on certifiable properties of machine learning systems. It extends the theoretical unlearning methods proposed in earlier papers by implementing them in a unified framework and introduces new mechanisms for certification, bridging the gap between theoretical proposals and practical deployment.
enables: This systematic evaluation establishes a foundation for developing unlearning methods that balance practical constraints in real applications. By clarifying the efficiency-effectiveness-certifiability trade-offs on linear models, the work enables future research to extend these methods to more complex model classes while maintaining certified guarantees. The unified evaluation pipeline provides a standard benchmark for assessing new unlearning proposals. The certifiability framework developed here could inform the design of verifiable unlearning mechanisms for nonlinear models, neural networks, and other architectures. The empirical insights also guide algorithm design for scenarios with privacy regulations, making principled unlearning more feasible in deployed systems where data deletion requests must be honored efficiently while maintaining model integrity.
